name: AU Student Government
description:
The American University Student Government is open to all students and serves as the preeminent student organization on campus. We are committed to enhancing the student experience by representing and furthering the interests of the entire student body through advocacy, accessibility and transparency. 

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: AUSG is the preeminent student organization at American University dedicated to enhancing the student experience by representing and advocating for their interests and creating quality programming.

name: AU Club Council
description:
AUCC is a recognized, chartered student governance organization that is directly funded by the undergraduate Student Activity Fee and is dedicated to building stronger clubs at American University by supporting them with all the resources and expertise at our disposal. We provide funding to all recognized undergraduate clubs on campus, resources needed for clubs to engage the student body, and organizational and leadership development to help clubs and their leaders grow.

We envision a responsive and engaged AUCC as the cornerstone of a successful, inclusive, collaborative, financially responsible, and transparent student organization network that both strengthens and enriches the AU community and represents the university positively off-campus.

Looking for your Club Consultant? Check out the documents section below!

To schedule an appointment with a team member, please send an email or check auclubcouncil.com/meettheteam for their calendar link.

General Inquiries & Club Consultants: aucc@american.edu

Chair: aucc-chair@american.edu

Finance: aucc-financedirector@american.edu

Engagement & Communications Director: aucc-commdirector@american.edu
summary: AUCC provides funding to recognized undergraduate clubs on campus, resources needed for clubs to engage the student body, and organizational and leadership development for clubs to be successful on campus.

name: Student Media Board
description:
Student Media Board is composed of leaders of each student media organization: AmLit, ATV, The Aviary, AWOL, The Blackprint, The Eagle, EGO, Photo Collective, Second District Records, and WVAU. Each organization is completely student-run and open to all AU students.

 

Student Media Board is open to all students. 

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: Student Media Board is the governing board for the 10 student media organizations at American University.

name: Kennedy Political Union
description:
Housed under the Student Activity Council (SAC), the Kennedy Political Union (KPU) is the non-partisan central political programming board at AU. As an organization, we bring the most politically relevant and impactful speakers to engage the AU community and focus on important issues across the political spectrum. KPU provides impactful dialogues around current political issues, curating meaningful conversations.

---

  • The Kennedy Political Union is open to all students.
  • American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: The Kennedy Political Union is the non-partisan central political programming board at AU.

name: Student Union Board
description:
Housed under the Student Activity Council (SAC), the Student Union Board (SUB) is the entertainment bureau on campus that strives to bring artists, actors, and other creatives by curating events that are pursuant to the values of the AU community. While representing the diversity that defines AU, we aim to express the interest of a wide range of students. Our goal is to bring free-spirited events to the well-deserving students.

---

  • The Student Union Board is open to all students.
  • American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: The Student Union Board is the entertainment bureau on campus that strives to bring artists, actors, and other creatives by curating events that are pursuant to the values of the AU community.

name: Students for Change
description:
Housed under the Student Activity Council (SAC), Students for Change (SFC) helps coordinate and advise on programming and policy decisions that affect the marginalized AU community. The programming board creates and delivers responsive, effective, and educational events to address issues such as gender, sexuality, anti-violence, anti-racism, environmental & climate justice, queer & trans inclusion, wellness, and more.

---

  • Students For Change is open to all students. 
  • American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: Students for Change helps coordinate and advise on programming and policy decisions that affect the marginalized AU community.

name: Center for Student Involvement
description:
The Center for Student Involvement (CSI) is the hub for the student experience at American University. CSI facilitates leadership development, organizational advising, and programs that help foster a sense of belonging for the next generation of changemakers.

 

CSI advises the following governance cohorts:

• American University Club Council (AUCC)

• American University Student Government (AUSG)

• Fraternity & Sorority Life (FSL)

• Graduate Leadership Council (GLC)

• Student Activities Council (SAC)

• Student Media Board (SMB)
summary: The Center for Student Involvement is the hub for the student experience at American University. CSI facilitates leadership development, organizational advising, and programs that help foster a sense of belonging for the next generation of changemakers.

name: Muslim Student Assocation
description:
MSA at AU is a safe community for all Muslims and non-Muslims on campus interested in learning about and applying the principles in the Quran and Sunnah. MSA at AU is comprised of diverse and welcoming individuals who work to offer a non-judgmental space to practice one’s faith. Members can also participate in programming and advocacy initiatives supporting AU's Muslim community. MSA at AU is open to all students.

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: MSA at AU is a safe community for all Muslims and non-Muslims on campus interested in learning about and applying the principles in the Quran and Sunnah.

name: American Literary Magazine
description:
AmLit is a literary publication comprised of poetry, prose, photography, art and short film submitted by the AU campus community and is published biannually at the end of the fall and spring semesters. The magazine is available both in print and online.

Aside from publishing a magazine each semester, we aim to foster an inclusive, creative community on campus by hosting events throughout the year. While many of us are writers and artists, you do not have to be creative to join the AmFam. We welcome all kinds of people with every different major under the sun, with any level of experience and time commitment! Open to all students.

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: American Literary Magazine, or AmLit, is American University's student-run literary and visual arts magazine.

name: American University Television
description:
ATV is the only video-based media organization on campus, focusing on broadcast television and creative projects. All of our programs are hosted, produced, edited, and managed by students. We have three branches, news, sports, and creative, where we strive to produce professional journalism and film. 

  • ATV is open to all students. 
  • American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

Check out our Instagram @auatv for more info!
summary: ATV is American University's student-run television station. Enjoy our programming, and join us today!

name: American Way of Life
description:
AWOL aims to continue pushing ourselves and American University to report critically on issues that deserve to be understood with nuance; to work subversively when dismantling barriers that suppress certain voices; and to love irrepressibly when it comes to serving our community. We ignite campus discussions on social, cultural and political issues. We want to make our campus more inquiring, egalitarian and socially engaged. Apply to work with us!

Our stories have an angle, which is different from having an agenda. Our reporting is impartial and fair.

We were independently founded by American University students in 2008. While we are still student-run, today we are housed under AU's Student Media Board. AWOL is a member of the Associated Collegiate Press and Generation Progress Voices Network. This publication has won awards at the National College Media Convention, and its writers have won awards from the Society for Professional Journalists and the College Media Association.

Open to all students. American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: AWOL is a student-run magazine and online news source dedicated to subversive, investigative reporting.

name: The Blackprint
description: The Blackprint is an online and print publication at American University. We serve as a platform open to all students for raising minority issues and an amplifier of marginalized voices. As a part of the student media board, our primary goal is to inform people of what’s going on in their school community, keep them up to date with the latest cultural happenings, cover local events, and highlight the work of student leaders. We invite all minds and majors on this endless journey to stay honest, stay brilliant and #staywoke. American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: The Blackprint is an online and print publication at American University run by students of color and focused on community issues.

name: The Eagle
description:
The Eagle publishes content daily across multiple platforms and prints two special editions per semester. It has a staff of 115 and a number of leadership roles and different sections that are open to all students.

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: The Eagle is AU's award-winning student-run newspaper that is open to all students and publishes daily content online and prints two special editions per semester.

name: The Aviary
description:
While the Aviary centers the experiences of women and non-binary AU students, membership is open to all students interested in its mission. Our publication aims to promote a welcoming, inclusive environment for students of all interests and areas of study to engage in journalism. Our goals include allowing all voices to be heard, and we aim to give our writers an opportunity to be published. The Aviary is committed to creating a safe and diverse environment for the AU community. We are open to all students, so come join us!

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: The Aviary serves as a journalism-focused safe space for women and non-binary AU students and welcomes AU students of all backgrounds to its community.

name: Photo Collective
description:
AU Photo Collective is a vibrant community of artists from all walks of life. Whether you’re eager to learn photography, master model posing, or explore film and videography, we warmly welcome you. Regardless of your skill level or equipment, we invite you to join our events and connect with our supportive network. We also offer a camera rental service, featuring a variety of camera bodies and lenses for you to experiment with! Feel free to reach out if you have any questions and we hope to see you all at a PhoCo event soon!

 

Photo Collective is open to all students. 

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: Photo Collective is a community of student photographers, filmmakers and visual artists.

name: WVAU
description:
With a staff of over 120 student DJs, WVAU offers unique musical programming and a perspective that you won’t find anywhere else. Our staff brings you daily articles and music reviews that you can find on WVAU.org and our DJs offer shows streamed online all day long. Outside of the studio, WVAU brings in local and touring artists every semester for interviews, in-studio performances, and our Capitol Boogie free concert series. All of our events are free and open to the public.

WVAU is open to all students. American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

Feel free to join our slack if you're interested, or follow our Instagram (@wvau) for updates!
summary: American University's student-run, internet-only radio station

name: Second District Records
description:
The DMV is known for its 'First District' – the domination of politics and international relations.

But there is, however, a 'Second District,' one that people fail to recognize – the heavy, vibrant music scene that has come to represent the area in various ways.

We at SDR want to embrace the music, talent, and diversity of the DMV – we believe in the power of being different 

and open to all students.

name: Alpha Sigma Phi
description:
Alpha Sigma Phi is a fraternity interested in one thing and one thing only -- to create better men, who are able to succeed in all things academically and socially and to learn lessons of respect, brotherhood, and responsibility that will carry throughout their lives. We are an organization that is dedicated to creating bonds that last a lifetime, and our mission is to raise up all our members to the highest possible moral standards to ensure that we take in only the highest caliber of young men seeking a Greek life experience.

We have chosen to define our Fraternity as a co-curricular organization to underscore our desire to partner with the institutions that host our chapters in their endeavor to prepare their students for success after college.
summary:
Alpha Sigma Phi is a collegiate men's secret and social fraternity with 180 currently active chapters. Founded at Yale in 1845, it is the 10th oldest fraternity in the United States.

We are the Beta Chi chapter at American University

name: African Students' Organization
description:
Our purpose is to expose Africans and non-Africans to the beautiful continent and all that it has to offer. This organization is open to and welcomes all AU students. Our organization aims to celebrate African culture and raise awareness of contemporary matters through education and communal efforts.

We, as a dance team, aim to showcase African culture in hope of raising awareness about the beauty of the African continent, its rich traditions, its influence in the black diaspora and we are open to all students. American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: Les Coeurs D'Afrique (Hearts of Africa) promotes African/Diasporic culture through dance. We aim to showcase African culture to raise awareness about the beauty of the African continent, its rich traditions and its influence in the black diaspora.

description:

The NAACP is open to ALL people.

name: South Asian Student Association
description:
The purpose of the South Asian Student Association is to extend and promote South Asian culture at American University. We serve as a safe space for individuals and would like to foster community on AU's campus through holding social, professional and dialogue based events pertaining to South Asian experiences and cultures. Our organization is not exclusive to South Asian members and is open to all students.

name: Asian American Student Union
description:
The Asian American Student Union (AASU) exists to promote cultural awareness, understanding, and appreciation of the Asian American community at American University and beyond. We work to amplify voices through advocacy on issues impacting the Asian American community, their allies, and the broader AU community.

AASU strives to provide a safe, inclusive space for all students to express themselves freely and build meaningful connections of like-minded individuals committed to cultural exchange.

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

name: Caribbean Circle
description:
This organization is open to all students. It shall be the purpose of the Caribbean Circle to serve as an active educational vehicle for the American University by promoting the social, cultural, and political aspects of Caribbean life, and presenting issues from a Caribbean perspective. The Caribbean Circle shall also be dedicated to promoting unity among the Caribbean students and the entire American University community.

description:
The BRASA AU is dedicated to the promotion and appreciation of Brazilian culture in all of its forms within the American University (AU) community. BRASA AU aims to:

  1. Assist members to develop both academic and professional connections.
  2. Serve as a community for Brazilian and non-Brazilian students to connect and facilitate cultural adjustments to campus life.
  3. Open to all students

name: Black Student Union
description:
The Black Student Union is an inclusive, student-led organization dedicated to uplifting and supporting students at American University. Guided by our Five-Point Program, we strive to promote advocacy, unity, and personal growth among all individuals and diverse cultural backgrounds. Through engaging events, meaningful dialogue, and community-based programming, we create space for connection, celebration, and empowerment.

While our mission centers on the experiences of Black students, BSU is open to all students who are interested in learning about, engaging with, and supporting the Black community. Whether you're looking to build community, expand your perspective, or become involved in campus advocacy, there's a place for you in BSU.

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status
summary: We are an organization comprised of and dedicated to serving Black students at American University. The purpose of our organization is to support the social and political aspects of all Black identities through advocacy and programming.

name: Ethiopian and Eritrean Student Association at American University
description:
EESA at American University is a philanthropic and cultural organization that serves two purposes:

  • Give back, by giving money and giving time, to organizations that support both Ethiopia and Eritrea and allies locally and globally.
  • Promote the Ethiopian and Eritrean culture on campus and connect with the community and all communities in the DMV area.

EESA is open to all students.

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: The purpose of EESA at American University is to work to promote awareness of the Ethiopian and Eritrean community on campus

name: Graduate Leadership Council at American University
description:
The Graduate Leadership Council (GLC) is the representative body for the more than 2,500 graduate students at American University (AU). The GLC brings together the leaders of the six Graduate Student Councils at AU to mobilize on issues that are relevant to graduate students specifically and the AU community generally.

It is the mission of the GLC to ensure that your time as a graduate student is as enjoyable as possible by providing social networking events and professional development opportunities, as well as advocating for your concerns directly to the University Administration. 

The Graduate Leadership Council is open to all students.

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

name: Phi Sigma Kappa
description: Phi Sigma Kappa is more than just a fraternity; it's a cohesive brotherhood dedicated to fostering academic excellence and character development. We pride ourselves on creating a supportive environment where members hold each other accountable, encourage personal growth, and engage in meaningful service. Our chapter actively participates in campus events and community service projects. In the last year we have raised money for a local breast cancer awareness foundation along with a transitional housing organization based in DC. Join us to experience a community that values scholarship, character, and brotherhood.
summary: Phi Sigma Kappa, Epsilon Triton, is the oldest Fraternity on AU's campus, founded on November 15th, 1936.

name: Pi Kappa Alpha
description: The Kappa Upsilon Chapter of Pi Kappa Alpha is dedicated to developing men of integrity, intellect, and high moral character and to fostering a truly lifelong fraternal experience. Pi Kappa Alpha fosters an uplifting community in which its members strive both socially and academically while creating amazing networking opportunities for post-grad with over 305,000 initiates around the world. Around American University and Washington DC, brothers contribute to philanthropic efforts to better our community.
summary: For the establishment of friendship on a firmer and more lasting basis.

name: Sigma Alpha Epsilon
description: What is now the largest North American Greek-letter social fraternity, started out with only eight friends at the University of Alabama, in Tuscaloosa, Alabama, on March 9, 1856. These eight men were united by the common goal of creating an organization based upon the highest standards of friendship, scholarship, and service, all qualities they felt should be embodied by “a true gentleman.” Though founded during a tumultuous time in our nation’s history, the bonds of brotherhood proved strong in the proud members of Sigma Alpha Epsilon (SAE).
summary: The Washington City Alpha chapter of Sigma Alpha Epsilon was officially chartered in April 2017. SAE is the largest fraternity in the nation with 212 active chapters and over 240,000 living alumni.

name: Sigma Alpha Mu
description:
Welcome to the Sigma Alpha Mu, Delta Beta Chapter page! Our fraternity is dedicated to making a positive impact both within the American University (AU) community and beyond. We focus on fostering a supportive brotherhood, academic excellence, and community service.

Our main philanthropic efforts include:

  • The Judy Fund: This fund supports Alzheimer's research, aiming to find a cure and improve the lives of those affected by this condition.
  • Armor Down: Founded by a Delta Beta alum, Armor Down assists veterans in readjusting to civilian life, providing them with the resources and support they need for a successful transition.

We invite you to learn more about our chapter and join us in our mission to make a difference. Feel free to come by and say hello anytime!
summary: Hi! We are Sigma Alpha Mu or Sammy for short. Our fraternity is dedicated to fostering lifelong friendships, academic success, and leadership development.

name: Sigma Chi
description:
The Fundamental Purpose of Sigma Chi is the Cultivation, Maintenance, and Accomplishment of the Ideals of Friendship, Justice, and Learning within our Membership.

Sigma Chi cultivates a strong bond within its fellowship, offering prospective members a chance to gain lifelong friendships and connections. We offer interested students a chance to meet our brothers each year during our rush, which is held in both the fall and spring of each year. Interested students should feel fee to come to events which will be posted on our Instagram page!

Within the AU community, Sigma Chi is a strong presence in the engagement of all greek philanthropies. We also hold our philanthropy, "Derby Days", which raises thousands of dollars each year to contribute towards cancer research at the Huntsman Cancer Research Institute.
summary: Social Fraternity

name: Sigma Phi Epsilon
description: Sigma Phi Epsilon is a community for academically morally driven men, offering a space for members to grow as balanced men. Benefits of our organization include our semesterly retreat, along with philanthropy with the American Foundation for Suicide Prevention (AFSP). As a member, one can expect participation in the numerous intramural sports on campus, multiple philanthropy events, and a strong connection to the SigEp community.
summary: The DC Delta Chapter of the Sigma Phi Epsilon Fraternity at American University. An organization committed to fostering personal and academic growth among its members, inspiring community leadership, and forging lifetime bonds among its membership.

name: Zeta Beta Tau
description:
ZBT prides itself on being an inclusive Jewish organization welcoming of any college man who understands and appreciates our Mission. ZBT will recruit and initiate men of good character — regardless of religion, race or creed.

Founded in 1898, ZBT is the world’s first and largest Jewish Fraternity. Since our historic founding, more than 140,000 men have been initiated into our great Brotherhood. Again making history, in 1989 ZBT became the first fraternity to abolish pledging and all two-tier membership statuses. Our Brotherhood focuses on equal rights, privileges and responsibilities for all brothers. We firmly stand against all forms of hate in our communities, and we are working to create a safer and more inclusive college and community environment for all students.

ZBTs can be found in all aspects of life: business, entertainment, media, politics, tech, philanthropy and more.
summary: Founded in 1898, ZBT prides itself on being an inclusive organization welcoming of any who understands and appreciates our focus on creating a safer and more inclusive college and community environment for all students.

name: Zeta Psi
description: The Delta Chi Chapter at American University was originally founded in 1969 by a group of like-minded students looking for a fraternity that promoted civic and campus engagement while also eschewing hazing practices. The original incarnation of the chapter was short-lived, as the Vietnam War and Anti-Greek movement of the early 1970s led to a number of chapters closing, including the Delta Chi chapter. The chapter was refounded in 2009 by a group of friends who similarly eschewed the negative aspects of Greek culture, while also wanting an organization that was more inclusive. Today the Delta Chi chapter of Zeta Psi strives to be a fraternity for people from all walks of life, seeking to build friendships and become better men.
summary: A global fraternity sustained by love and lifelong friendships since 1847, Zeta Psi members recruit and develop authentic, honorable men, loyal to creating a better brotherhood and better communities.

name: Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity Inc.
description: The Nu Beta Chapter of Alpha Phi Alpha prides itself on representing the goals and mission of Alpha Phi Alpha Fraternity Inc., developing leaders, promoting brotherhood and academic excellence while providing service and advocacy for our communities.
summary: The Nu Beta Chapter of Alpha Phi Alpha Fratnerity, Inc.

name: Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority Inc.
description:
Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority, Incorporated® was founded on a mission comprised of five basic tenets that have remained unchanged since the sorority’s inception more than a century ago. Alpha Kappa Alpha’s mission is to cultivate and encourage high scholastic and ethical standards, to promote unity and friendship among college women, to study and help alleviate problems concerning girls and women in order to improve their social stature, to maintain a progressive interest in college life, and to be of “Service to All Mankind".

 

The Lovely Lambda Zeta Chapter of Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority, Inc. was chartered on the campus of American University on February 26th, 1977 by 19 women. Since our chartering, Lambda Zeta has continued to uphold a legacy of excellence. We are committed to serving all mankind by implementing campus and community programs and through numerous service efforts in the Washington D.C. area.
summary: Alpha Kappa Alpha Sorority, Incorporated is a globally-impactful organization bound by the bonds of sisterhood and empowered by a commitment to servant-leadership that is both domestic and international in its scope.

name: Kappa Alpha Psi Fraternity Inc.
description: Kappa Alpha Psi Fraternity, Incorporated was founded January 5th, 1911 on the campus of Indiana University at Bloomington, Indiana. The American University Chapter, the Kappa Chi of Kappa Alpha Psi was charted September 12th, 1981. Kappa Chi encompasses American University, Catholic University, Georgetown University, and The George Washington University. The fundamental purpose of Kappa Alpha Psi is achievement. Since it's inception, Kappa Alpha Psi, and Kappa Chi Specifically has been dedicated to service in the community, especially in D.C.
summary: The Kappa Chi (KX) Chapter was chartered on the campus of The American University on September 12, 1981. The KX Chapter is a city-wide chapter that includes Georgetown, George Washington, and Catholic University in addition to The American University.

name: Latinas Promoviendo Comunidad/ Lambda Pi Chi Sorority Inc.
description:
The first Latina-focused sorority founded at an Ivy-league institution, Latinas Promoviendo Comunidad /Lambda Pi Chi Sorority, Inc. was established on April 16, 1988, at Cornell University. The Epsilon Chapter was founded jointly between American University and Georgetown University on November 23, 1996

Our actions give life to the ideals we hold dear to our hearts. But, it is through our Vision, Mission, and Goals that we conceptualize who we are as an organization and as Hermanas.

Vision

A lifetime network of Hermanas dedicated to empowering themselves and their communities.

Mission

To empower women by providing a supportive network dedicated to their personal and professional advancement. Our Hermandad is further advanced by our shared dedication and promotion of public service and cultural awareness, with an emphasis on Latino history, contributions, and experiences.

Goals

  • To unify women through the promotion of the Latino community and the Latino culture;
  • To promote community service to the local community;
  • To work towards the advancement of all people of color with a special emphasis on the Latina;
  • To enlighten our respective communities about Latino cultural, social, and political issues;
  • To sponsor cultural events which identify and evaluate the existing and emerging roles of women with an emphasis on the Latina; and
  • To promote professional advancement by encouraging academic excellence, mentorship, and networking.

Ideals

As a Latina Greek-lettered organization, we aim to promote and improve our communities. We also strive to be successful in our personal and professional endeavors. For this reason, our Founders encompassed all these goals when determining the ideals of our Hermandad. They chose La Comunidad, La Cultura Latina, and La Hermandad.

name: Zeta Phi Beta Sorority Inc., Sigma Pi Chapter
description:
Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Incorporated, is a historically Black Greek-Lettered Organization (BGLO), aimed at promoting the advancement of collegiate women. Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Incorporated, was founded on Friday, January 16th, 1920, at Howard University in Washington, DC. The organization began as an idea conceived by five coeds to address the societal mores, ills, prejudices, poverty, and health concerns of the day. Our Five Pearls, Arizona Cleaver Stemons, Pearl Anna Neal, Myrtle Tyler Faithful, Viola Tyler Goings, and Fannie Pettie Watts, dared to depart from traditional coalitions for Black women and sought to establish a new organization predicated on the precepts of Scholarship, Service, Sisterhood, and Finer Womanhood. Over the years since the sorority’s inception, Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Incorporated, has chartered hundreds of chapters and initiated thousands of women around the world. Zeta has continued to thrive and flourish while adapting to the ever-changing needs of a new century. 

The Sophisticated Sigma Pi Chapter of Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Incorporated, was chartered on Friday, October 14th, 1998, on the campus of American University in Washington, DC by five women. Since then, the chapter has expanded to Trinity Washington University, Georgetown University, and The Catholic University of America. The chapter strives for excellence through exceptional campus leadership, a strong academic record, and everlasting sisterhood. Our chapter members are strong and intelligent women who come from diverse backgrounds and incorporate Finer Womanhood into every aspect of their lives. Additionally, our goal is not only to serve our prospective school campuses and communities but to build long-lasting relationships around the world.
summary: Zeta Phi Beta Sorority, Incorporated, is a community-conscious, action-oriented organization, dedicated to addressing the societal ills of the day, while also empowering young women to take action through national initiatives and partnerships.

name: The Alexander Hamilton Society
description:
Who We Are

 

The Alexander Hamilton Society (AHS) is a non-partisan, not-for-profit, national organization that seeks to identify, educate, and launch young men and women into foreign policy and national security careers imbued with the Hamiltonian perspective of strong and principled American leadership in global affairs.

 

Founded in March 2010 by Professor Aaron Friedberg of Princeton University, Daniel Blumenthal of the American Enterprise Institute, and Roy Katzovicz of Saddle Point Group LLC, we were established to confront U.S. foreign, economic, and national security policy failures at their source — universities — and to provide our students with the experiences they would otherwise not gain, in all the fields from which they will influence U.S. policy. We supply what American higher education lacks: vital, constructive programming that is critical to the intellectual and professional development of a new generation of American leaders. With the world more chaotic and dangerous today than it has been in a half-century, we seed the ground for the future of American foreign policy.

 

What We Believe

 

An appreciation that the world remains a dangerous place in which our power must be exercised with prudence, and where the primary threats to our security come from states that deny freedom to their own people and from non-state actors who embrace hatred and violence;

 

A conviction, rooted in the history of the last century, that the world is a better, safer, and more prosperous place when the United States is willing and able to lead; and a commitment to maintaining the moral authority and material strength on which that leadership rests;

 

A measured pride in the success of the American experiment; an understanding that America’s greatness is the result of its commitment to individual liberty, limited government, economic freedom, the rule of law, human dignity, and democracy; and a belief that the fundamental aim of every aspect of our policy, foreign and domestic, must be to defend these principles at home and ultimately to encourage their spread abroad;

 

A clear recognition that, in such a world, our true friends and reliable partners are other democratic nations with whom we share an enduring commonality of values, and not simply a temporary convergence of interests;

 

A firm belief that, time and again, in peace and in war, the ability of the American political system to profit from vigorous public discussion has proved its worth; and that, at this moment in our history, our public discussion of foreign, economic, and national security policy stands very much in need of renewal.

The Alexander Hamilton Society is open to all students. American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: The Alexander Hamilton Society (AHS) is a non-partisan, non-profit organization that prepares the next generation of leaders for careers in foreign policy and national security, guided by the Hamiltonian vision of strong, principled American leadership.

name: Gospel Choir at American University
description:
History 

The American University Gospel Choir has existed on the AU campus for more than three decades. In 1976, Valerie Yarborough and students Linda Jackson, Sheryl Taylor, Steven Wright, and Star Jones had a burning desire to sing gospel music and ring out shouts of praise. Under the spiritual guidance and advising of Rev. Clarence Cross, the choir quickly began to grow with students from various ethnic and religious backgrounds. Gospel spring revivals were organized and brought in preachers from all across the eastern seaboard. At these revivals, choirs from all over the city came and gave witness to the old time gospel songs and revival styles. 

The choir’s theme is derived from Psalms 150: 6 —

“Let everything that hath breath praise ye the Lord…”. We ask all to pray that we continue to uplift the name of the Lord to all who will listen.

Motto

Gospel means good news. Our commitment to the gospel of Jesus brings joy, positivity, and strength to our community. American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status. Open to all students.

name: Mock Trial @ AU
description:
American University Mock Trial competes against other college teams within the American Mock Trial Association (AMTA). Each academic year, AMTA releases one criminal or civil case packet to every college team in the country. The case packet includes all the necessary materials to try the case in a simulated legal trial. Auditions are open to all students.

Teams study the legal documents, the exhibits, and the witness affidavits in the case packet as they prepare to try the case for both the plaintiff/prosecution and defense. AMTA tries to make their cases as balanced as possible, giving plenty of evidence for both the plaintiff/prosecution and the defense to use. The art of mock trial is presenting the evidence in the most persuasive and realistic way possible in order to win points from the judges.

Mock Trial @ AU is open to all students. name: Students for a Just Society
description: Our organization was born out of frustration and a sense of overwhelming unfairness. Many of us are students studying justice and law at American University, and we found ourselves angry with the state of the criminal justice system as we learned about it in our classes. We wanted a way to truly enact change in our community and beyond. Students for a Just Society is an organization fighting for justice in the United States by educating our peers, amplifying the work of other justice organizations, and encouraging in-depth dialogue between academics in the field, students with passion and ideas, and officials in the justice system. We hope to make complicated issues in the American justice system more accessible to the general public, so that everyone can better understand the root of the existing problems and the proposed methods of solving those problems. We are a nonpartisan and research-based organization aiming to get everyone involved in criminal justice change at any level. Students for a Just Society is open to all students. We often encountered classmates who would respond with a look of curiosity and lack of awareness upon hearing about our backgrounds. This lack of knowledge of our homeland among our peers inspired us to take action. By forming this club, we aim to promote awareness and appreciation of Albania’s rich culture, history, and heritage within our university community. We wish to provide a platform for individuals interested in Albanian culture to connect and engage in meaningful cultural exchanges. Our goal is for this club to contribute to a more inclusive and tolerant university community, by dispelling any misconceptions and stereotypes about Albania, as well as by creating intercultural communication between our peers. name: Center for Leadership & Community Engagement
description:
CLCE (Center for Leadership & Community Engagement) leads AU in its commitment to advancing social justice and civic responsibility.  CLCE is at the front lines of community engaged-work through the following programs:

We support any member of the AU family trying to become engaged in social issues. Alternative Breaks offer domestic and international field experience to students and staff, while our DC Reads program allows students to improve literacy in Washington, DC. Our Freshman Service Experience pairs hundreds of incoming students with DC’s many nonprofit organizations, connecting these students – through service – with their new home and the issues facing it.

Over 3000 AU undergraduate students volunteer each year, and there’s still more work to be done. If you’re already a member of the AU community, we invite you to help advance our campaign for social justice and civic engagement. If you’re considering AU for your studies, we hope you’ll join us at a university that truly puts ideas into action and action into service.

We are a multicultural and multi-theoretical staff who deeply value our global and diverse community. We take a stand against discrimination and oppression in any form. name: Sunrise Movement at AU
description:
Welcome to Sunrise American University! We are beyond excited that you are interested in becoming a part of our team, and we can’t wait for you to become a leader in the movement for climate justice. We are open to all students on campus. To get started with us, please fill out this form and check out our New Member Guide: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1kEHF7h_CygpX4rznkxruc_TDojq-eH-GMo8JICOVH3M

Who We Are

The Sunrise Movement is a revolution of young people whose goal is to stop climate change and create millions of good jobs in the process. Sunrise American University is an autonomous chapter of this national movement. Acting under the movement’s fundamental principles, we work to build the sunrise community on and around campus. We do this by working with other hubs, supporting other activists, and holding our own actions.

Becoming a Leader

Most importantly, Sunrise AU is a platform where you are able to amplify your voice in the name of Sunrise. We are a community that serves as a resource for you as a leader, so we encourage you to take action when you have an inspired idea or interest. As long as it abides by the movement’s principles, we encourage you to take action for climate justice!

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

name: Office of Sustainability
description:
American University is committed to having a small footprint, promoting environmental education, and acting on climate change. Join us on AU’s sustainability journey! Everyone can get involved and we need YOUR help make our campus a greener, healthier, and more sustainable place for everyone.

 

GET INVOLVED

  • Volunteer with the EcoRep Program to promote sustainability and inspire personal behavior change across campus. 
  • Become certified through the EcoPledge Program, earn prizes, and commit to taking sustainable action on campus and beyond.
  • Intern with us and help advance the goals in AU's sustainability plan.
  • Properly sort your waste into AU's landfill, recycling, and compost bins.
  • Join an environmental student organization.

 

FIVE FAST FACTS

  1. AU was the first university in the U.S to achieve carbon neutrality.
  2. AU has divested all public fossil fuel investments from its endowment.
  3. AU offers campus-wide composting as part of our Zero Waste program.
  4. All 84 acres of AU’s campus is an accredited arboretum.
  5. In 2021, AU released a new five-year sustainability plan for campus.

 

CONNECT WITH US

  • Follow @thegreenau (Instagram) and @greenau (Twitter and Facebook).
  • Sign up to receive the Office of Sustainability’s sustainability newsletter.
  • Email sustainability@american.edu with any questions, ideas, or inquiries. 


Please note: Our membership roster on Engage is limited to undergraduate students who are participating in our EcoRep Program. Please subscribe to our newsletter or follow us on social media to stay in touch.
summary: American University is committed to having a small footprint, promoting environmental education, and acting on climate change. Get involved with the Office of Sustainability and help make our campus a greener and healthier place for all.

name: Disabled Student Union at American University
description:
DSU is committed to dismantling ableism and interlocking forms of oppression while building community. From advocacy across the DMV to community care for disabled students on campus, we welcome all students to join us in pushing for and practicing disability justice. We have no end goal, for disability justice will never be complete. We enact collective access and intersectional thinking to welcome all students to DSU. We aim toward a friendly, compassionate world where ability doesn’t define worth in our advocacy and in the social belonging we foster. As we promote change at the university and around the DMV area, we will enlist the help of disabled students at AU, disabled staff and faculty at AU, and allies and accomplices to the disability community. Along with our physical meetings and actions, we are committed to providing online ways to engage with our club, like social media content and hybrid meetings, as we create community through cross-disability solidarity.

All materials for the sessions are provided by us and you can always ask us to provide new materials you're interested in- whether that be for painting, fiber arts, collages, or sculpture! name: The Pericles Institute at American University
description:
The Pericles Institute is the first and only undergraduate-run foreign affairs think tank based at American University. Named for the great Athenian general during the Peloponnesian War, we seek to embody his keen strategic thought and admiration for our democratic system. 

 

Between international conflict, emerging technology, and public health emergencies, the world we live in faces many contemporary challenges. These issues come amidst rising global tensions and a lack of diplomatic cooperation, reminding us of the need for thoughtful and prudent leaders. The Pericles Institute aims to sharpen the next generation of diplomats, analysts, and scholars to address those pressing global challenges. We publish op-eds from our fellows under a broad definition of “international relations,” alongside periodic events, guest speakers, and professional development.

The Pericles Institute is open to all students. American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

name: Young Democratic Socialists of America at American University
description:
Young Democratic Socialists of America at American University, or YDSA @ AU, is a space for like-minded AU students to come together and be empowered to fight against the most pressing social justice issues impacting our communities. 

We educate our fellow students on how capitalism is negatively impacting every social justice issue from climate change to racial justice to universal healthcare, and to organize campaigns that will combat these threats in order to improve the lives of everyone at AU and the broader community.

YDSA @ AU practices radical solidarity with other progressive AU organizations and local groups that are fighting for justice, equality, and liberation. Our goal is to push for true democracy on AU's campus to better serve as the ground for a democratic socialist future.

Members of YDSA @ AU come from all different walks of life, yet we all share a fundamental passion for democratic socialism, community organizing, and political activism.

YDSA is open to all students. American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

name: It's On Us At American University
description:
It's On Us at American University works to create a community in which students and survivors feel safe from sexual violence on campus. To do so, we plan to educate students on: sexual assault prevention techniques, how to stay safe on campus, how to report sexual assault/harassment, bystander intervention, and about the Title IX process. Additionally, we engage in advocacy for survivors and ending sexual assault as well as rape culture on campus. This organization strives to promote a safe space on campus for all survivors regardless of sexual orientation, race, and gender identity. By educating students about sexual violence and assault, we hope to foster a safer community that encourages students to report sexual violence, feel comfortable with the resources available on campus, and destigmatize rape culture at American University.

It's On Us at American University is open to all students. American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

description:
Consult Your Community is open to all students.

Consult Your Community is a nationwide, student-run nonprofit organization that empowers America’s top undergraduate students to leverage their knowledge of business with their passion for service. Students engage with low-income and minority-owned small businesses over the course of a semester through an innovative program in which they are trained to provide business owners with comprehensive, pro bono consulting services (teams are advised by business school faculty, graduate student mentors, and experienced professionals from the top-tier consulting firms). Through this process, students gain hands-on business experience and develop skills that prepare them for their future careers, while business owners become empowered to foster their own economic independence and secure their future prosperity, both of which contribute directly to our nation’s economic development and long-term sustainability.

name: Sine Institute
description: The Sine Institute is a nonpartisan Institute of Politics (IOP) at American University. Our mission is to bring all voices to the table and create a space where those with a variety of perspectives, experiences, and beliefs can convene, communicate and collaborate, addressing the issues that impact us all. Through our Sine Fellowship and Sine Distinguished Lecturer programs, we host free, on-campus events with prominent speakers throughout the year. Our Fellows and speakers have included Members of Congress, governors, members of the media, advocates, activists, astronauts, actors, professional athletes, and many others. We also conduct research on Gen Z in politics and other areas by conducting an annual poll of young Americans ages 18-34, uncovering new information on their perspectives regarding political leadership, higher education, technology, AI, and other critical topics. Whatever your interest areas are, we want to meet you! Please stay tuned and help spread the word about our programs, speakers, research, and upcoming events.  “Juris Mentem,” translating to “Legal Mind,” is the name of American University’s student-run Law Journal overseen by the School of Public Affairs. Juris Mentem seeks to provide students with the opportunity to improve their legal research and writing abilities through our biannual publication.

As a community, Juris Mentem is charged with providing students an opportunity to learn and practice the ability to engage in scholarly legal discourse. description:
The purpose of this organization shall be to

  • Connect all of the AU students - open to all AU Students - with real world professional statisticians and data scientists.

The American Statistical Association is the world’s largest community of statisticians that support excellence in the development, application, and dissemination of statistical science. As an ASA student chapter at American University, we provide opportunities for students to connect with prominent statisticians and data scientists, as well as encourage students to continue studying statistics and provide career information in statistical and data science.

Our mission is to engage AU students' learning skills on Data Science and Statistical Methods and to understand how to leverage the power of data and statistics in a real-world environment by using different tools, technology, and mechanisms such as Statistical Analysis, Data Visualization, Artificial Intelligence, and Machine Learning. Our goal is to advance students as future data scientists and statisticians by providing opportunities to learn, grow and build their network with professionals.

name: Changing Aid Signature Research Initiative
description: The aid sector is in a pivotal moment of transformation triggered by the COVID-19 pandemic, the war in Ukraine, and increasing demands for donors, recipients, and aid organizations to “decolonize” and “localize” aid. AU’s Changing Aid Signature Research Initiative (SRI) grapples with these challenges by providing a unique space for aid actors, scholars, and policymakers to come together to learn, communicate, and problem-solve. The Changing Aid initiative spearheads innovative, ethical, and participatory research within the most challenging contexts, such as migration, violence, peace, healthcare, and even disastrous environmental and climate changes. We engage with a variety of aid actors (both international and domestic, from the Global North and South, and from bilateral, multilateral, non-governmental, and private institutions) and facilitate difficult discussions and practical training to help aid workers better manage the challenging realities of aid work today. Informed by evidence and in partnership with policymakers and aid industry actors, we work to help find solutions to improve and inform future aid.
summary: Understanding and transforming aid in violent, insecure, and crisis-affected environments.

Welcome to the Animal Rights Coalition of American University! We are a group of individuals who are passionate about making the world a kinder, more respectful place. We are voices for the voiceless, advocating for the end of factory farming and animal abuse. 

Animal Rights are not something we can ignore in today's world. Beyond respecting animals' rights to a life without violence, animal agriculture has profound effects on climate change and consuming animals has links to several major health concerns. 

This club has the goal of educating those around us, as well as creating a space for other like-minded individuals to connect. We are very excited you are interested in joining us on our mission for a cruelty-free world! Open to all students!
summary: We are here to create awareness and educate American University and the greater DMV area about issues that stem from factory farming, including animal rights, environmental impacts, health issues, and overconsumption.

name: ADVANCE - First Generation Student Organization
description:
ADVANCE: Empowering First-Generation Law Students at WCL

ADVANCE is WCL’s only mentorship program geared toward the retention of first-generation law students. We define “first-generation” as students who are the first in their immediate family to graduate high school, university, or attend law school in the United States. Created and run by peer, we seek to support and elevate the academic and professional success of first-generation law students. Recognizing the unique challenges these students often encounter, ADVANCE provides structured guidance and mentorship to help them confidently navigate law school and the legal profession.

 

Core Goals of ADVANCE:

  • Improve academic performance through skill-building and mentorship.
  • Transform faculty into key allies by encouraging meaningful student-professor engagement.
  • Support students throughout the job application process with targeted resources and advice.
  • Promote effective time management and a balanced, healthy approach to law school.
  • Help students fully leverage WCL resources to maximize their law school experience.

 

ADVANCE is more than mentorship, it’s a commitment to equity, empowerment, and building a strong foundation for long-term success in the legal field. 


Note: While our mission is to support students who are the first in their immediate family to go to law school, we seek to support all peers. Participation in this ADVANCE is open to all members of the AUWCL community, regardless of background. Our events, programs, and activities are inclusive and do not restrict participation based on personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: Affinity group for first-generation college and law students.

name: Christian Legal Society
description:
The American University Washington College of Law’s Christian Legal Society (CLS) is dedicated to serving Jesus Christ through the practice and study of law. CLS seeks to fulfill God’s command found in Micah 6:8 to “act justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with God.” We do this by inspiring, encouraging, and equipping Christian attorneys and law students, both individually and in community, to proclaim, love, and serve Christ through their law practice. By providing mentors and resources to law students, we endeavor to foster spiritual growth and show others the love of Christ in our words and actions. We seek to provide a safe community where law students from all denominations and backgrounds feel welcome. 

 

AUWCL CLS objectives:
1. To honor Christ by loving, supporting, and encouraging our neighbors.
2. To proclaim Jesus as Lord through all we do.
3. To encourage Christian attorneys to view law as a ministry and help them integrate faith and their legal practice.
4. To provide Christian attorneys a means of society, fellowship, and discipleship.
5. To provide a forum for discussing issues related to Christianity and the law.
6. To provide a safe community where law students from all denominations and backgrounds feel welcome.  

 

Participation in our organization and its AUWCL events is open to all members of the AU community, regardless of background. Our events, programs, and activities are inclusive and do not restrict participation based on personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

 

***For Spring 2026 - weekly Bible studies/general body meetings are Tuesdays from 5 PM - 5:50 PM in Room NT03, (around the corner from the ceremonial classrooms). We also meet on Thursdays at Noon in the Atrium.

name: American University Law Review
description:
Founded in 1952, the American University Law Review is the oldest and largest student-run publication at the American University Washington College of Law (WCL) and publishes six issues each year. As WCL’s flagship law review, the Law Review is ranked among the top law journals in the United States, according to the Washington and Lee University Law Library.

The Law Review receives approximately 1,500 submissions annually and publishes a wide range of legal scholarship from professors, judges, practicing lawyers, and renowned legal thinkers. The Law Review has published articles or commentary by Supreme Court Chief Justices Warren Burger, William Rehnquist, and Earl Warren, as well as Associate Justices Hugo Black, Ruth Bader Ginsburg, and Arthur Goldberg. The Law Review has also published articles or commentary by prominent legal figures such as Stephen Bright, Paul Butler, Erwin Chemerinsky, Tom Goldstein, Paul Kamenar, Judge Paul Michel, Judge Stephen Reinhardt, Nadine Strossen, and Laurence Tribe.

In addition to serving as an academic forum for legal scholarship and a research tool for professors and practitioners, the Law Review is committed to developing the writing and research skills of its staff. The Law Review showcases student-written pieces by publishing notes and comments, unrestricted in terms of subject or area of the law, in each issue. In support of the staff, the Law Review has an esteemed faculty advisory board, including Professors Ira P. Robbins, Elizabeth Beske, Walter Effross, Christine Haight Farley, Andrew Guthrie Ferguson, Paul Figley, Angi Porter, and Stephen Wermiel.

The Law Review is the only journal in the nation to publish an annual issue dedicated to decisions of the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit regarding patent law, international trade, government contracts, veterans affairs, and trademark law. A member of the National Conference of International Law Journals, the Law Review is also indexed in LexisNexis, Westlaw, HeinOnline, the Index to Legal Periodicals, and the Resource Index/Current Law Index. Each edition of the Law Review is distributed nationally and abroad to law school libraries, private law firms, public legal organizations, and individual subscribers.

With more than seventy years of publications, the Law Review has a strong national and international alumni network, including judges and practitioners in every field.

The Law Review also houses an online companion publication called the Forum. The Forum serves as a platform to generate timely discussion of scholarship published in the Law Review’s print issues. It enables the academic discourse within the Law Review’s print issues to continue after publication. The Forum also creates more opportunity to advance student-written scholarship by publishing notes and comments written by Law Review staff members. Like the print publication, the Forum is available on HeinOnline, Westlaw, and LexisNexis, as well as through the Law Review’s website.

name: American University Business Law Review
description:
The American University Business Law Review ("AUBLR") is the first law review in Washington, D.C., dedicated solely to business issues. Originally launched as a brief, AUBLR is now ranked #15 in Business, Corporations, and Securities Law journals by the Washington and Lee Law Journal Rankings.

AUBLR is committed to publishing high-quality, impactful scholarship from both established and emerging legal scholars. It seeks to offer cutting-edge legal analysis and propose innovative solutions to the complex challenges that businesses face today. As the first business law publication in the nation’s capital, AUBLR also sponsors an annual symposium where leading scholars and practitioners convene to discuss pressing issues in business law.

Edited and produced by students at American University Washington College of Law, AUBLR’s thirteen volumes encompass a wide range of business law scholarship, reflecting the latest legal developments. AUBLR welcomes article submissions from academics, practitioners, and law students, with a rolling review process for potential publication.

name: National Security Law Brief
description:
Founded in April 2009, the American University National Security Law Brief (NSLB) is the nation’s first student-run law school publication to focus on the rapidly evolving field of national security law.  The publication is an annual online publication with a complementary blog component, devoted to examining the legal dimensions of United States national security law and policy.  In addition to analyzing traditional security issues such as counterterrorism, intelligence collection, and nuclear proliferation, the Brief also examines broad legal matters such as soft power, technology, cybersecurity, immigration, and sovereignty.

​

The NSLB’s inaugural print issue was released in the Summer of 2010, and contained national security law articles written by accomplished professors and practitioners, as well as analytical pieces written by current students at American University’s Washington College of Law.  The NSLB welcomes numerous points of view on national security law issues and encourages students, academics, and practitioners to submit feedback and articles for submission.  Given our location in Washington, DC and our growing relationship with the legal community, we know that the NSLB will continue to serve as an ideal platform for new ideas in national security law.

 

Participation in NLSB and its AUWCL events is open to all members of the AU community, regardless of background. Our events, programs, and activities are inclusive and do not restrict participation based on personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

name: Sustainable Development Law & Policy Brief
description:
The Sustainable Development Law and Policy Brief (SDLP) is a student-run publication at AUWCL. SDLP is affiliated with the Program on Environmental and Energy Law (PEEL) and collaborates with other associated student organizations.

 

 

Participation in SDLP and its AUWCL events is open to all members of the AU community, regardless of background. Our events, programs, and activities are inclusive and do not restrict participation based on personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

Established in 1997 in collaboration with the Center on International Environmental Law. The publication has hundreds of subscribers worldwide. With the support of the faculty and dedicated students at American University Washington College of Law, SDLP publishes two issues per year, both online and in print. Each publication highlights articles and notes by practitioners as well as students. Through the novel legal arguments and distinguished authors, SDLP has become a well-known and respected publication throughout the environmental and sustainable law field.

 

Our mission is to serve as a valuable resource for practitioners, policy makers, and concerned citizens promoting sustainable development, environmental preservation, environmental justice, and biodiversity throughout the world. Because our publication focuses on reconciling the tensions found within our ecosystem, it spans a broad range of environmental issues such as sustainable development; trade; renewable energy; human rights; air, water, and noise regulation; climate change; land use, conservation, and property rights; resource use and regulation; and animal protection.

WHY JOIN?

  • It is an amazing opportunity to get published in law school! Staffers can publish a feature or full-length article.
  • Staffers will sharpen their legal writing, research, and bluebook citation skills, it is great preparation for Journal if you choose to join.
  • Staffers will have opportunities to network with individuals in the sustainability and environmental law field. We have an incredible network of Alums in the DC area and across the US, including international environmental law attorneys.
  • Our annual symposium gathers academics and practitioners from the public and private sectors to discuss recent developments in the sustainability and environmental law field.
  • SDLP fosters an amazing community of like-minded students through close relationships between staffers, faculty, and alums through social gatherings and events!

name: Transactional Negotiation Team
description:
The Transactional Negotiation Team is a student-run organization that strives to empower law students to excel in the boardroom. Members participate in national, regional, and intra-school competitions, and, in doing so, transform knowledge from the classroom to practical abilities. TNT works closely with the Business Law Program at AUWCL to prepare members to become successful advocates. To achieve this mission, TNT works with Intellectual Property Law Society (IPLS), Business Law Review (BLR), and the J.D./MBA programs to host events discussing trends and popular issues in transactional law while TNT assembles and prepares teams to compete in negotiation and transactional competitions. All business specializations within the broad practice of transactional law are embraced by TNT.

 

As a competitive face of WCL, TNT welcomes 2Ls, 3Ls, and 4Ls to compete in domestic and international regulatory, business, legal drafting, and negotiation competitions. TNT encourages 1Ls to join the society in the spring semester of their 1L year and take part in mooting with current TNT competitors to prepare the various teams for upcoming competitions. Members are required to participate in one competition per year. Members earn up to 2 credits per academic year, make lasting connections, and are provided various scholarship and professional opportunities transactional law. Ultimately, TNT members gain knowledge, flexibility, adaptability, experience, and expertise in negotiation skills, oratorical skills, legal and policy drafting, and teamwork.

 

Note that membership in this Engage page is only for active members of TNT but registration for TNT events is open to all students.

Over 25 Years of Promoting Accountability for Atrocity Crimes through Teaching, Training, Research, and Advocacy

AUWCL established the WCRO as part of its commitment to promoting and enforcing human rights and humanitarian law. For 25 years, the WCRO has served as a critical resource for tribunals and other organizations promoting accountability for atrocity crimes at the international, regional and domestic level. At the same time, it has offered students an unparalleled opportunity to engage in projects promoting accountability for these crimes, helping students build essential legal and advocacy skills and establish connections critical to practicing in the fields of international criminal law and international justice. The WCRO complements this work through educational and advocacy initiatives, including the Hague Summer Program, its War Crimes Speaker Series, participation in conferences and workshops, research and publications.

name: SGI Buddhist Chanting
description:
SGI-USA is part of a global community-based network that practices the humanistic philosophy of Nichiren Buddhism centered on respect for the dignity of life. We are the most diverse Buddhist community in the United States.
As members of the SGI, we are committed to dialogue and nonviolence based on the conviction that individual happiness and the realization of peace are inextricably linked.
What is the way to genuine and lasting happiness? Buddhism teaches that we develop true happiness by bringing forth our inherent goodness, or Buddhahood, not only for our own well-being but also for the greater good of humanity. Our daily practice is centered around chanting Nam myoho renge kyo. Nichiren Buddhism offers a way for all of us to reveal our innate Buddhahood and actualize our limitless potential in this lifetime.


Our weekly meetings provide a space for dialogue, chanting, sharing experiences and learning. Please join us Monday evenings at 7 PM in the Kay Spiritual Life Center Lounge.
summary: SGI-USA is part of a global community-based network that practices the humanistic philosophy of Nichiren Buddhism centered on respect for the dignity of life. We are the most diverse Buddhist community in the United States.
