name: Advocates for Underrepresented People in Law
description: American University’s Advocates for Underrepresented People in Law endeavors to promote and represent any AU student interested in the legal profession. AUPL’S main purpose is to build a community uniting all individuals, which will be cultivated through the emphasis of inclusivity. Various social events and gatherings will serve to further develop the organization, available for all AU students considering the legal field. AUPL events will be centered around networking with other AU students, practicing attorneys and current law students. Through a panel discussion composed of guest speakers from diverse backgrounds, attorneys and law students will share their journey. AUPL strives to educate itself and its members on the unique challenges the guest speakers have faced, and how they have overcome said challenges. Through their guidance, club members will be inspired and further educated on the legal profession. Our organization additionally intends to host workshops that center on job searching, for example, researching fellowships and internships. A direct emphasis will be placed on job openings that urge marginalized individuals and their allies to apply. The Executive Board will act as an advisor to club members, advising during the stages of the application process, to further build their credentials. While the work of AUPL centers the experiences of marginalized groups and communities, including individuals of minority backgrounds, the LBTQ+ community, those of various religions or beliefs, gender identity, individuals with disabilities, and all socioeconomic statuses, AUPL is open to all students interested in our mission. Let it be known that the organization is accessible to all students at American University, as every student is encouraged to join and further advocate for equality. Ultimately, AUPL aspires to draw awareness of the current inequalities faced by underrepresented individuals in the current legal world, effectively addressing disparities and providing representation for students.
summary: American University’s Advocates for Underrepresented People in Law strives to empower and stand for any AU student interested or wishing to pursue law. AUPL’s primary goal is to address and combat inequalities existing in the contemporary legal sphere.

name: Association for Wargaming at American University (GLC)
description:
How to Get Involved

If you are interested in joining our events, join this Discord! Other useful links are at the bottom.

discord.com/invite/ZeqBWTkFC5

 

Our Mission

AWAU provides professional development opportunities to the next generation of security professionals. 

 

We use wargames to teach new skills and develop a deeper understanding of today’s global security environment. Many of the wargames we run are also used by the US national security enterprise to prepare its people to confront today’s threats.

 

In addition to running wargames, we host conversations with subject matter experts and provide events access to conversations with the US’ national security leaders. Some of the people that we have previously brought to campus include the Vice Commander of SOCOM, the Director of HSI, and the Director of the US Marshals Service.

 

What is wargaming?

Wargaming is a simulation activity that helps professionals and students prepare by simulating real-world situations at the strategic and tactical levels. Wargames do this by creating a structured environment that provides feedback on how specific actions impact the operating environment and influence outcomes. Through iterative gameplay, users can improve their decision-making skills, better understand the operating environment, and improve strategies.

name: Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Inc., Nu Alpha Chapter
description:
History

Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated was founded on January 13, 1913, by 22 collegiate women at Howard University to promote academic excellence and provide assistance to those in need. The Founders of Delta Sigma Theta envisioned an organization committed to sisterhood, scholarship, service, and addressing the social issues of the time. Since its founding, Delta Sigma Theta has become one of the preeminent service-based sororities, with more than 300,000 initiated members and over 1,000 chartered chapters worldwide.

As a sisterhood comprised primarily of Black, college-educated women, the Sorority seriously considers the issues impacting the Black community and boldly confronts the challenges of African Americans and, hence, all Americans. Over the years, a wide range of programs addressing education, health, international development, and the strengthening of African American families have evolved. The major programs of the Sorority are based upon the Five-Point Programmatic Thrust: Economic Development, Educational Development, International Awareness and Involvement, Physical and Mental Health, and Political Awareness and Involvement. 

Mission Statement

Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Incorporated is an organization of college educated women committed to the constructive development of its members and to public service with a primary focus on the Black community.

Chapter History

The Nu Alpha Chapter of Delta Sigma Theta Sorority, Inc. was chartered on April 22, 1976, by twelve dynamic students:  Deborah Barnes, Andrea Blackwell, Yvette Britt, Diane Carter, Angela Green, Deborah Green, Vera Johnson, Antoinette Jones, Sharon McEaddy, Sherill Morton, Jacqueline Scott and Jacqueline Wyatt
summary: Delta Sigma Theta, Nu Alpha was previously chartered at American University in 1976 and will be rejoining the Intercultural Greek Collective. Please contact us directly with any questions at the contact email below.

name: Association for Wargaming
description:
Join our Discord server! https://discord.gg/NTc8bVT4xz

 

Our Mission:

AWAU promotes the professional practice and hobby of wargaming to students and community members. Through gameplay, discussion, and guest lectures from wargaming professionals, our programming educates members on the professional applications of games and simulations. We also run collaborative events with external wargaming organizations to provide networking opportunities and expand understanding of the utility and limitations of professional wargames.

 

What is Wargaming?

"A wargame is a model involving people making decisions in a synthetic environment of competition or conflict, in which they see the effects of their decisions on that environment and then get to react to those changes." -Peter Perla

Our programming focuses on board wargames. We explore historic, future, and fictional conflicts from the strategic to the tactical level. Some of the games we play are Twilight Struggle, Eclipse: Second Dawn for the Galaxy, and Breaking the Chains. No experience is necessary, come prepared to learn!

 

The Association for Wargaming is open to all students. It is through devotion to the ideals of compassion, courage, diversity, innovation, integrity, professionalism, and service that we better both our profession and our community. Through accepting our differences we become not just a law fraternity, but a family.

Our Purpose

The purpose of this Fraternity shall be to form a strong bond uniting students and teachers of the law with members of the Bench and Bar in a fraternal fellowship designed to advance the ideals of liberty and equal justice under law; to stimulate excellence in scholarship; to inspire the virtues of compassion and courage; to foster integrity and professional competence; to promote the welfare of its members; and to encourage their moral, intellectual, and cultural advancement; so that each member may enjoy a lifetime of honorable professional and public service.

name: The Pre-Law Society at AU
description:
The Pre-Law Honors Society aims to build a community of interested and prospective pre-law students, by networking with professionals in all areas of law, under different practices. Our mission is to show students the inner workings of the practice of law within government, public interest, and private practice. The Pre-Law Society is dedicated to fostering academic and professional excellence by providing students with opportunities to develop legal writing skills, connect with law schools and professional associations, and participate in events designed to encourage discussion and build practical, résumé-ready skills such as mock constitutional arguments, motion writing, and trial simulations. Our main focus is to help AU students who are interested in a pre-law track, not only network and gain membership from current attorneys, but also make sure students interested in law school have the proper resources to succeed in applications. This includes, but is not limited to, practicing for LSAT’s, internship opportunities, and volunteering across the D.C. community. The ultimate goal of the Pre-Law Society is to help pre-law students gain invaluable experience and knowledge ahead of their law career, and to ultimately give back to our community in return. The role of the executive board in the Pre-Law Society is to provide a firm hand to help guide students in the right direction. We aim to build strong connections and mentorship opportunities between students and law schools to guide members through the law school admissions process and help them prepare for the academic and professional journey ahead. We strive to deepen students’ understanding of the legal profession, enhance their prospects for law school admission, and support them in making informed decisions about whether to pursue law school and how to navigate the process successfully. The Pre-Law Society also aims to create a lasting relationship between the club and local law firms in the Metropolitan area. American University is fortunate to be in the center of the legal field. In the Pre-Law Society we want to use this resource, and create these relationships with local law firms, associations, law schools, judges, and attorneys, so that our members feel confident exiting law school and into the job market. The Pre-Law Society is open to all students. 

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.

name: Collegiate Institute for Middle Eastern Affairs
description:
The Collegiate Institute for Middle East Affairs (CIMEA) is a national nonpartisan educational organization dedicated to advancing students’ understanding of the Middle East and Africa. We seek to empower the next generation of informed leaders to engage thoughtfully and constructively with complex international challenges. We recognize that the rising hostility and polarization that characterizes many student spaces today undermines the very purpose of higher education. Our work is guided by the conviction that campuses should be places of curiosity, dialogue, and mutual respect rather than conflict, silence, and polarization. We strive to meet students where they are and equip them with the tools and opportunities necessary to become the most informed, capable, and empathetic versions of themselves.

​

CIMEA serves as a meeting ground for students of all backgrounds to explore a wide range of topics concerning the Middle East and Africa. We are dedicated to the free exchange of ideas, and see meaningful participation in the exchange as our starting line. In this way, we believe the free exchange of ideas and the inclusion of diverse perspectives are essential to international affairs dialogue on campuses across the United States. By fostering open, informed debate and offering transformative opportunities for exposure and development, we cultivate leaders who are prepared to mediate complex global dialogue in their own communities and to lead the conversations that shape foreign policy tomorrow.

Open to all students.

American University student organizations shall not discriminate in membership or benefits on the basis of any individual’s personal identity or characteristics, including but not limited to race, color, national origin (or shared ancestry), religion, sex, age, sexual orientation, disability, or veteran status.
summary: The Collegiare Institute for Middle Eastern Affairs is American University's a 501c(3) non-profit, think tank, and professional development organization focused on creating future leaders in the realm of Middle Eastern and North African affairs.

name: American University Core
description: The AU Core Curriculum, American University's inquiry-based liberal arts education, challenges students to put their curiosity to use inside and outside of the classroom. The AU Core fosters the agile thinking that prepares students to engage in a complex, constantly changing world. The AU Core begins with a first-year experience and foundational skills, highlights essential habits of mind, then integrates these skills and habits with the student's major, and culminates in a capstone. At each stage, the AU Core offers sustained attention to equity, diversity, and inclusion.

name: United Against Inequities in Disease
description:
United Against Inequities in Disease (UAID) at American University is a student organization dedicated to reducing health disparities in our local D.C. area by leveraging the power of research and developing community-based initiatives. Through a community health needs assessment, we identify key health needs through comprehensive data collection. By collaborating with local non-profits and organizations, we create a sustainable project to address the inequity found in our research. On campus, UAID works to advocate by hosting discussions and speakers to raise awareness of health disparities. We also present opportunities for students to receive health equity certificates, leadership training, and attend networking events.
